text: This is a robotic repair of a recurrent paraesophageal hernia with a toupee fundoplication. The patient is a seventy-two year old female with a history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and gastroesophageal reflux who is status post laparoscopic repair of a paraesophageal hernia with Nissen fundoplication approximately fourteen months prior. She initially did well after surgery, however, then developed persistent dysphagia and recurrent reflux symptoms. A barium swallow showed significant stasis of oral contrast in the barium tablet held up at the GE junction, as indicated with an arrow. An upper endoscopy revealed a loose wrap with a pouch below the GE junction with retained food in the stomach. These findings combined with the patient's symptoms were suggestive of a slipped nissen.

The patient is positioned supine with arms out, and an epigastric Nathanson retractor is used to lift the left lobe of the liver, exposing the gastroesophageal junction and the angle of His. A small type II paraesophageal hernia is visible, along with a replaced left hepatic artery. The Nissen fundoplication is observed to have slipped distally over the stomach, with sutures detached from the esophagus. The hernia sac is located above the wrap anteriorly.

The robotic arms are docked, and the surgeon uses a forced bipolar device and monopolar curved scissors to carefully dissect and remove adhesions from the wrap. The three previously placed fundoplication sutures are identified, dissected, and divided. The hernia sac is then dissected free from the diaphragm and anterior wrap and subsequently divided.

The surgical team uses a 14-inch Penrose drain to encircle the gastroesophageal junction, providing gentle traction. They continue the dissection to take down the previous wrap, noting the dilation of the anterior hiatus while the posterior sutures remain intact. The esophagus is further mobilized circumferentially.

The surgeon achieves adequate mobilization of the esophagus, obtaining five to six centimeters of intra-abdominal length. The posterior crura are closed using a 2-0 Ethibond suture, with each stitch tied with four knots. An additional anterior suture is placed to prevent esophageal kinking.

The surgeon mobilizes the fundus and passes it posterior to the esophagus. A 58 French bougie is inserted, and a 270-degree Toupee fundoplication is performed using three sutures. The wrap leaflets are secured to the posterior crura with one stitch. The superior suture includes the esophageal muscle, fundus, and superior crura, while the second inferior suture connects the esophageal muscle to the fundus. Both the anterior and vagus nerves are identified and protected during the procedure.

The surgeon positions the fundoplication above the gastroesophageal junction, ensuring proper placement. The bougie is carefully removed, and the surgical field in the left upper quadrant is checked for complete hemostasis.

An upper endoscopy is performed intraoperatively, confirming the wrap is positioned above the z-line. The retroflexion view shows a 270-degree wrap. The surgeon retrieves the needles and Penrose drain, removes all instruments, and undocks the robot, with no visible esophageal or gastric injuries.

The total operative time was one hour and four minutes. Post-operatively, the patient was monitored on telemetry overnight on the surgical floor and allowed clear liquids. On step day one, the patient was advanced to a pureed diet and discharged home that afternoon.
